description: This figure displays the room-temperature engineering stress–strain curves for low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels containing 0.5 wt% C (black curve), 0.8 wt% C (red curve), and 1.2 wt% C (green curve). The horizontal axis represents engineering strain and the vertical axis gives engineering stress in MPa. Visually, the 0.5C steel exhibits the lowest flow stress and fractures at the smallest strain. The 0.8C curve rises to the highest peak stress before necking, while the 1.2C curve sustains strain to the largest elongation with a slightly lower peak stress. The caption reports that the 0.8C steel reaches the highest ultimate tensile strength of about 979 MPa, whereas the 1.2C steel attains the greatest total elongation of 56.2% and a UTS × total elongation product of 53.2 GPa%, nearly double that of the 0.5C steel. These curves collectively demonstrate that increasing carbon content enhances both yield strength and ductility, with peak strength at the intermediate 0.8% C composition and the optimal strength–ductility balance achieved in the 1.2C alloy.
